title: Tribus
subtitle: A 3D kid in a 2D town, holding a single rose and a lot of hope.
specs: A 3D character trapped in a 2D world

gallery.caption: Platforming through DUOLAND — the two-dimensional town that never quite accepted him.
Floating fragments and a long way down.
The world is flat. Tribus, stubbornly, is not.

body: Meet Tribus: a happy little kid living in the extremely happy 2D town of DUOLAND, where everyone gets along wonderfully — except him. Tribus has a problem, which is that he is three-dimensional. In a flat world. He's more advanced than everyone around him and the universe simply cannot cope, so he's spent his whole life left out and friendless. Relatable, honestly.

Then he hears that Amare — a girl in the village — loves roses. If he can bring her a Roseract for her birthday, maybe, just maybe, he'll finally have a friend. So off he goes. That's the game: a lonely 3D boy platforming across a 2D world for one shot at not being alone.

The whole design leans on that one contrast — 2D world, 3D protagonist — and it does a lot of quiet emotional lifting. Being the thing that doesn't fit the space around it turns out to be a great platforming mechanic and a decent metaphor. We made it with the Toroka crew for Mağara Jam 3 and landed 5th, which for a melancholy jam game about a sad geometric child felt like a win.

I was on development — making a character who obeys 3D rules feel right inside a 2D stage was exactly the kind of "wait, how do we even do this" problem I love. Tribus is early-days me, but the instinct is already there: take a weird technical constraint and let it carry the whole feeling of the game.
